title: Hunger, Tiredness, Loneliness — the HALT Check

hook.rwenLine: Most 'I'm in a bad mood' moments are actually 'I haven't eaten, I haven't slept, and I haven't talked to anyone real today.' HALT is the four-letter check.
hook.culturalNote: HALT — Hungry, Angry, Lonely, Tired — comes out of recovery communities (AA / Al-Anon) but the underlying observation is older: basic physiology drives much of what we mistake for psychology. The check is free.

chunks.native: Hungry — Angry — Lonely — Tired. Four states that distort everything else if unmet.
chunks.literal: Recovery community heuristic; also folk wisdom going back centuries
chunks.phonetic: Run the check before you trust the mood.

chunks.native: If you're in HALT state, postpone the decision. The state will pass; the decision can wait.
chunks.literal: Daniel Kahneman on physical state and decision quality
chunks.phonetic: Don't fight on an empty stomach.

chunks.native: Eat. Drink. Lie down. Text someone. THEN reassess.
chunks.literal: Maslow basics; also therapeutic practice — physiology before cognition
chunks.phonetic: The list is short for a reason.

pattern.name: Run HALT before you trust the story.
pattern.explanation: When you're suddenly furious / hopeless / catastrophising / about to send a sharp message — run HALT. Hungry? Angry (and at what)? Lonely? Tired? Often two boxes light up. Address those first, then reassess. Half the moods that wreck a day dissolve with food, water, twenty minutes flat on a couch, and one human voice.

examples.target: Wanting to quit your job at 11pm
examples.native: You're tired. Decide nothing. Sleep. Reassess Wednesday.

examples.target: Snapping at a partner over the dishwasher
examples.native: You're hungry and lonely. Eat. Hug. Try again.

examples.target: Convinced everyone's against you in the group chat
examples.native: Tired and isolated. Phone a friend who actually likes you. The thread will look different.

exercises.instruction: Match mood to common HALT cause

exercises.question: Best time to run the check?

options.text: Only when in a clearly bad mood
Before any big decision OR strong reaction OR difficult message
Once a day at the same time

exercises.explanation: HALT is reactive — you run it when something feels off. It's also great as a daily standing check around 3pm when the dip lands for most people.

exercises.sentence: Run HALT before you trust the _____ .

exercises.question: Which of these would HALT likely help with?

options.text: A clinical depression that's been going for months
A 4pm mood crash that arrives nearly every day

exercises.explanation: HALT is for state-shifts that the body causes. Clinical conditions and grief have their own logic and need more than a sandwich. Both still benefit from not skipping meals.

rwenDialogue.intro: Right now — let's run the check.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Hungry? Tired? Lonely? Anything brewing as angry?

userChoices.feedback: Then nothing big gets decided in the next hour. Eat first. Lie down for 15 if you can.
Then your mood is probably trustworthy. Carry on.
All four together is a full reset day. Cancel what you can. Eat, lie down, message one person.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: The check is two seconds. The decision you save yourself can be huge.

prompts.prompt: What do you do if you're in HALT?

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Run HALT at 3pm today. Note which boxes light up. Address whatever's lit — eat something, lie down, message someone — before you do anything that matters. Notice how the world looks 30 minutes later.

phase8.scenario: User has just run HALT. Rwen helps them act on whatever lit up — what would addressing it look like in the next 30 minutes?
phase8.successCriteria: User has named the lit boxes and committed to one specific action.
